question: Compare the top 3 open-source vector databases for a startup building RAG products.
answer: The top 3 open-source vector databases for RAG startups in 2025 are **Qdrant** (best for filtered search & cost efficiency), **Weaviate** (best for hybrid search & built-in embeddings), and **Milvus** (best for billion-scale deployments). All three are free to self-host, integrate with LangChain/LlamaIndex/Haystack, and offer managed cloud options — but they differ sharply in performance profile, operational complexity, and ideal startup stage.

key_findings.claim: Qdrant (Rust, Apache 2.0): Its ACORN algorithm integrates filtering directly into HNSW graph traversal, keeping filtered queries fast even when 99% of candidates are eliminated. It leads on raw performance (~2ms p99 latency, ~12,000 QPS) and runs on as little as a 4GB instance with millions of vectors using aggressive quantization. Qdrant Cloud starts at $25/mo with a free 1GB tier; ~$100–250/mo for 10M vectors — the most cost-efficient managed option.

Weaviate (Go, BSD-3): Ships with built-in vectorizers for OpenAI, Cohere, and HuggingFace, eliminating the need for a separate embedding pipeline. Its hybrid search merges HNSW vector similarity with BM25 keyword scoring in one query. Scales efficiently below 50M vectors but becomes memory-hungry above 100M. Managed cloud starts at ~$25/mo after a 14-day trial; ~$150–300/mo for 10M vectors.

Milvus (Go/C++, Apache 2.0): Fully disaggregated architecture separates compute and storage for independent scaling of reads, writes, and indexing — proven at billions of vectors with GPU acceleration. Largest open-source community with 35,000+ GitHub stars. Trade-off is operational complexity: self-hosting requires etcd and MinIO, and distributed mode is Kubernetes-native. Zilliz Cloud (managed) runs ~$150–350/mo for 10M vectors.
All three have dedicated LangChain integrations (QdrantVectorStore, Weaviate vector store, Milvus wrapper) and work with LlamaIndex and Haystack for building RAG pipelines.
For startups without large engineering teams, Qdrant and Weaviate offer better out-of-the-box simplicity; Milvus is reserved for teams with data engineering muscle targeting industrial scale.

limitations: Performance benchmarks (latency/QPS figures) vary by dataset, hardware, and configuration — treat as directional, not absolute
Pricing figures reflect 2025 estimates and may change; always verify current managed cloud pricing on vendor sites
Milvus DiskANN index support sourced from broader documentation rather than a single benchmark snippet
Chroma is excluded despite being popular for prototyping, as it lacks production-grade maturity for scaling RAG products
assumptions: The startup wants a fully open-source (self-hostable) solution, not a proprietary managed-only service like Pinecone
The comparison focuses on production RAG use cases, not just local prototyping (which would favor Chroma)
The startup may start small but needs a clear path to scale
next_steps: Run your own benchmark with your actual embedding model and dataset size — synthetic benchmarks only tell part of the story
Prototype with Qdrant first if you need fast filtered search on a budget; switch evaluation to Weaviate if you want to skip managing an embedding service
Consider Milvus (or its managed Zilliz Cloud) only when you project >100M vectors or have a dedicated platform/data engineering team
Evaluate hybrid search quality (recall@k with metadata filters) on your specific document corpus before committing
Check LangChain/LlamaIndex integration docs for whichever DB you choose to confirm compatibility with your RAG framework version

flagged_claims: Milvus supports GPU acceleration and multiple index types including HNSW, IVF, DiskANN
Weaviate Cloud starts at ~$25/month, $150-300/month for 10M vectors
All three integrate with LangChain via dedicated integrations
judge_verdict.reasoning: Most claims are directly supported by the provided snippets, but a few details are either only partially supported or not explicitly evidenced, especially Milvus index specifics, the exact Weaviate Cloud pricing phrasing, and the blanket claim about dedicated LangChain integrations for all three.
